With my current port, I feel like I can ignore the market for years and likely outperform

$MELI $META $AMZN $BN 😴

At 41x trailing earnings, some people quickly dismiss $MELI as being overvalued. I think this is a massive mistake.

Valuations are determined by two things, earnings growth and predictability/moat. Meli has both of these things.

In terms of growth, this company is about as good as you can get. Last quarter top line came in at 39% YoY, extending a 28 quarter streak of revenue growth of at least 30%. A company with this kind of sustained growth deserves a high trailing multiple to compensate for the future growth. Even with that high trailing multiple, Meli still only trades at 22x 2027 earnings.

It's also worth noting that management is not currently focused on maximizing profitability. Margins have shown slight compression because of this, which also contributes to a higher multiple when simply looking at earnings. The market has seen this reinvestment playbook before with $AMZN, which was often trading at earnings multiples over 50 over the last few years despite growing significantly slower than Meli.

The moat is also underappreciated. The entire business acts as a flywheel with each arm reinforcing the others, allowing one customer to be monetized in multiple ways. Once they enter the ecosystem it is very inconvenient to leave. I've gone more in depth on this in my previous Meli post, so I'll just leave it at this for now.

When you look at these factors together, as well as the sizeable TAM for Meli to still capture, the pathway for decades long compounding becomes clear. When put into perspective, suddenly a 41x multiple becomes very fair for this extremely high quality business.

$MELI 2030

$MELI is one of those businesses where the numbers look strong, but they don’t fully capture what’s happening underneath. So, what can $MELI be worth in 2030? 

Revenue is approaching ~$40b and still growing at a very healthy pace. If the business continues executing across commerce, payments, and credit, it’s reasonable to see revenue reaching something like ~80b by 2030. That implies growth decelerating from the mid 30s into the low teens, which is what you would expect at this scale.

The more interesting part is earnings. 2026 earnings are ~$60 per share, and growing quickly. As the business scales, more of the ecosystem starts to work together. Payments drives monetization, logistics improves efficiency, advertising adds high margin revenue, credit expands take rate, etc. 

From here, it’s reasonable to expect earnings to compound in the ~20–30% range over time. Not in a straight line, but as a general trend as the business matures and mix shifts toward higher margin segments. That gets you to something like ~$140 to $180 per share by 2030.

But even here, context matters. This is a business that reinvests heavily. Logistics, infrastructure, credit underwriting, and international expansion all require a lot of capital. That means margins won’t expand in a perfectly smooth way, and earnings will fluctuate depending on where they are investing at any given time.

Stock based compensation exists, but it’s not big, dilution is present, but more contained relative to the scale of the business. Retained earnings, on the other hand, are becoming increasingly important. At ~$60 per share today and growing fast, $MELI is generating serious earnings power. Over time, a meaningful amount of that is retained and reinvested back into the business. If those reinvestments continue to earn high returns, retained earnings become a major driver of long term value.

The balance sheet reflects that. As $MELI scales, its becoming more self funded. Cash generation improves, and capital can be redeployed into higher return opportunities like credit, logistics, and product expansion. At the same time, you have to watch the credit book, because that introduces a different kind of risk compared to a pure marketplace model.

So the question is not just how fast the business grows, but how well it allocates capital as it grows. From a valuation perspective, if $MELI is still compounding earnings at a healthy rate with strong returns on capital, a multiple in the ~20–25x range is reasonable. That would imply a valuation of roughly ~$3,000 to $4,500 per share by 2030.

$MELI started looking at this one again.

My high level read is that their revenue numbers are “artificially” high and heavily subsidised through their marketing & giveaways. 

The real measure of revenue is gross profit which is growing at a healthy 25-30%

No company is entirely immune to global economic struggles, but $MELI has built a highly sophisticated system to protect its core business and crush the competition. 

The global economy is currently facing a massive, supply-side energy shock. Geopolitical conflicts have restricted transit through the Strait of Hormuz, pushing Brent crude oil to near term highs. Commercial shipping fleets are being forced to take much longer routes around Africa, adding significant fuel costs and up to 14 days of delays to global supply chains. At the exact same time, stubbornly high interest rates and sticky regional inflation are heavily eroding the spending power of Latin American consumers.  
For a company that relies heavily on physical logistics to deliver over 2.4 billion packages a year, soaring fuel costs should be a fatal disaster. However, MercadoLibre survives this exact problem through the physics of network density. Because they process and deliver massive volumes of packages in highly concentrated areas, the physical distance and fuel consumed between each drop is incredibly low. They are also actively moving away from gas reliance by recently adding 10k electric vehicles to their fleet and utilizing smart lockers to aggregate local deliveries. When operating costs do climb too high, they leverage their market dominance to pass the inflation along. They recently raised seller shipping fees in Brazil by up to 21% to protect their own profit margins.

Consumers are feeling the economic pinch, but they have not stopped spending entirely. Instead, they are trading down and looking for better value on everyday essential items. MercadoLibre aggressively captured these shoppers by dropping its free shipping minimum in Brazil. Management is highly strategic here. They are gladly sacrificing temporary profits to lock in new, high-frequency buyers and build a wall that low-cost Asian competitors cannot climb.

Mercado Pago holds a staggering $12.5 billion loan portfolio. In a stagflationary economy, traditional banks typically suffer from massive spikes in loan defaults. MercadoLibre avoids this trap because they do not rely on lagging, outdated credit scores. They can monitor the real-time daily sales and purchases of the users within their closed-loop ecosystem, allowing them to underwrite credit safely with visibility into cash flows. Because of this unique data advantage, their default rate for credit cards actually dropped to a historic low of 4.4%.

They also directly offset their physical logistics costs by rapidly scaling their highly profitable digital businesses. Their digital retail advertising revenue surged by 67%. They are also deploying advanced AI that now successfully resolves 87% of Mercado Pago customer service interactions without human intervention, acting as a massive deflationary force on internal labor costs. Competing companies will be panicking if the macro continues to turn, but MercadoLibre is pouring $10.9 billion into Brazil and $3.4 billion into Argentina to build new fulfillment infrastructure. They are weaponizing the 2026 crisis to buy cheap assets and lock in an unstoppable, generational monopoly.

It goes without saying that a macro slowdown will slowdown MELI's growth too but they are positioned to benefit the most on the other side of it.

It is widely assumed that drastically increasing package volume will strain a logistics network and break delivery speeds. $MELI's 2025 fulfillment data proves otherwise.

Earlier this year, the company deliberately lowered its free shipping threshold to drive purchase frequency. Moves like this historically bottleneck supply chains by flooding the network with lower-ticket orders. But the strategy worked, and the total items delivered surged by ~36% YoY, jumping from 1.78B in 2024 to a massive 2.43B in 2025.

Yet, despite this massive influx of volume, their logistics network actually improved its efficiency. MercadoLibre successfully increased its 48-hour delivery rate from 72% in Q4'24 to 75% in Q4'25.

$MELI's hyper-growth accelerated because the 2020 pandemic forced Latin America online. Competitors targeted LatAm in 2019 precisely because digital adoption and internet penetration had finally reached a viable scale for capture. This dynamic does not break the 25 year consensus for MELI but validates it.

MELI spent two decades building the region's fractured payment and logistics infrastructure from scratch. When rivals flooded the market, MELI weaponized this deep, established network. It absorbed margin hits to lower free shipping thresholds and used its massive fintech arm to outmaneuver them. Foreign platforms capitalized on a newly digitized region, but MELI’s 25 year infrastructural head start is the exact reason it continues to capture more share of the market.
